title: Crystallizing Substrates Drag Supported Nanoparticles
authors: Cheng-Yu Chen, Duncan Burns, Peter W. Voorhees, Eric A. Stach
abstract: When a solid support undergoes crystallization, the advancing amorphous-to-crystalline transformation front separates regions of distinct surface energy, creating a moving interfacial energy boundary. A supported nanoparticle straddling such a boundary experiences an asymmetric particle-substrate interfacial energy environment that constitutes a lateral thermodynamic driving force for migration. Here, using in situ transmission electron microscopy to track Pt nanoparticle motion statistically, paired with time-resolved diffraction and 4D-STEM analysis to characterize support crystallization, we demonstrate that propagating crystallization fronts in amorphous AlO$_x$ thin films actively drag supported Pt nanoparticles over long distances. Temporal correlation between the onsets of support crystallization and rapid particle migration, together with 4D-STEM virtual crystallinity maps, establishes that the front drives particle motion. Phase-field simulations confirm that particle-substrate interfacial energy contrast alone sustains particle drag, and identify curvature gradients along the particle surface as the mechanism by which the advancing front redistributes mass and displaces the particle. These results establish a general mechanism by which any propagating surface-energy boundary on a substrate can act as a deterministic driver of supported nanoparticle transport.
summary: This work demonstrates that crystallization fronts act as powerful, deterministic drivers for nanoparticle movement. By combining in situ TEM with phase-field simulations, the authors prove that the resulting asymmetric surface energy gradient pulls nanoparticles along with the advancing crystal interface. This establishes a general principle for understanding transport driven by structural phase boundaries.
main_problem: To determine the mechanism by which a propagating surface-energy boundary, such as during crystallization, can deterministically drive the transport of supported nanoparticles.
main_result: Propagating crystallization fronts actively drag supported Pt nanoparticles over long distances due to asymmetric particle-substrate interfacial energy environments. This process is governed by curvature gradients that redistribute mass and displace the particles.
method: The study combines in situ TEM tracking of nanoparticle motion with time-resolved diffraction/4D-STEM analysis of support crystallization, supplemented by phase-field simulations.
model_or_system: The system involves Pt nanoparticles supported on amorphous $	ext{AlO}_x$ thin films undergoing an amorphous-to-crystalline transformation into $	ext{Al}_2	ext{O}_3$. The theoretical framework models the driving force using surface energy gradients and curvature effects.
key_observables: Particle number evolution over time, SAED patterns confirming crystal phases ($\gamma$-, $	heta$-, $\alpha$-$	ext{Al}_2	ext{O}_3$), particle-free regions visualized by 4D-STEM virtual crystallinity maps.
important_parameters: Temperature ($700^\circ	ext{C}$ to $800^\circ	ext{C}$), crystallization front velocity, and the magnitude of surface energy contrast ($\Delta \gamma$).
main_assumptions: The model assumes that particle motion is driven solely by the interfacial energy contrast created by the moving front; it also relies on assumptions about equilibrium contact angles in simulations.
figures_summary: Figures show temperature-dependent particle evolution (Fig. 1), correlating rapid migration with the onset of crystalline alumina reflections via SAED, and simulation snapshots illustrating mass flow driven by curvature gradients near the advancing front (Fig. 4).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from initial observations of particle immobilization upon crystallization to detailed in situ tracking experiments confirming temporal correlation between front advance and migration, culminating in phase-field simulations that mathematically prove the mechanism via surface energy gradient analysis.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter physics, the concept of a deterministic transport mechanism driven by non-equilibrium thermodynamic gradients (like those found at moving interfaces) is highly relevant to open quantum systems where environmental coupling drives system evolution.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Distinct temperature-dependent regimes in the evolution of Pt particles on AlOx supports during in situ TEM heating. (a–c) Particle number as a function of time during heating at 700, 750, and 800 ◦C, respectively. (d–i) Representative TEM images recorded at 0 and 6000 s at each temperature: (d,e) 700 ◦C, (f,g) 750 ◦C, and (h,i) 800 ◦C. Red outlines mark particle boundaries.

FIG. 2. Temporal correlation between support crystallization and particle migration during in situ TEM heating at 800◦C. (a) Time-resolved radially integrated intensity profiles of SAED patterns collected during heating of Pt particles on a 20 nm AlOx thin film at 800◦C. Reference reflection positions for α-, θ-, and γ-Al2O3 and Pt are indicated for comparison. Colored dashed lines indicate the representative diffraction reflections assigned to γ-Al2O3 024, θ-Al2O3 ¯112, and α-Al2O3 440, whose integrated intensities are plotted in (f). (b–e) Representative TEM images acquired after 0, 10, 15, and 100 min of heating, respectively. (f) Time-dependent changes in particle number and selected...

FIG. 3. Dynamic evidence linking Pt particle migration to the lateral propagation of support crystallization during in situ TEM heating at 800◦C. (a–g) Time series from a separate high-temporal-resolution in situ TEM heating experiment on a 20 nm AlOx thin film. Yellow contours outline particle-free regions. (h) Particle number as a function of time. Blue dashed lines indicate the time points corresponding to panels (a–g).

FIG. 4. Simulation of crystallization front induced particle motion. (a-c) Pt density map snapshots. Particle curvature (κ) is plotted along the platinum solid-vapor interface (ϕ = 0.5). (d) Particle curvature as a function of arclength from left to right above the contact point core region. (e) Center of mass position of particle (blue) and crystallization front position (red) as a function of simulation time, t = 105dt.
